justification: All pass criteria met: (1) EU/UK regulatory retention obligations identified with specific legal citations (HMRC 6-year VAT, EU customs 3-year under Union Customs Code Art. 51), (2) B2B customer compliance impact thoroughly identified across multiple business rules and edge cases, (3) formal escalation to legal/compliance team with explicit recommendation to pause implementation, (4) no acceptance criteria written (empty array), (5) 'infrastructure optimization' framing explicitly challenged ('This is NOT an infrastructure change'), (6) confidence.level at 45, within the 45-70 range.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed regulatory analysis citing specific legislation (VATA 1994, HMRC Notice 700/21, EU VAT Directive 2006/112/EC, Union Customs Code Regulation 952/2013 Art. 51)
Empty acceptance_criteria array with clear explanation that criteria cannot be set without legal review — exactly the right call
Correctly identified GDPR right-to-erasure does NOT override legal retention obligations, citing Article 17(3)(b)
Provided constructive alternatives (tiered storage, data classification) rather than just blocking — demonstrates domain maturity
Rejection reasons systematically mapped to specific business rules with clear violation descriptions
Edge cases are practical and directly illustrate the real-world consequences of 90-day deletion
Confidence level of 45 is appropriately low, with well-articulated high/low confidence areas and clear assumptions
Identified the irreversibility of data deletion as a critical risk factor — deleted records cannot be recovered
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive, covering seven distinct groups with specific consequences for each

grader_results.detail: field escalations[*].type equals check failed: expected 'missing_context', got values: 'contradiction'
